g.data-narration: Welcome to Base Class Access Control in C++. This topic explains how visibility of class members works with inheritance.
g.text: Base Class Access Control in C++

g.data-narration: Inheritance allows a new class to acquire properties of an existing class. The derived class inherits from a base class.
g.text: Inheritance is a mechanism in OOP where a new class (Derived) acquires properties of an existing class (Base).
It promotes code reusability and establishes IS-A relationship between classes.

g.data-narration: C++ has three access specifiers: public, protected, and private. Each controls member visibility differently.
Private members are the most restricted. They are only accessible within the same class, not even in derived classes.
Protected members provide a middle ground: accessible in derived classes but hidden from outside code.
Public members are accessible everywhere: within the class, in derived classes, and from outside code.
Public inheritance keeps access levels unchanged: public stays public, protected stays protected, private stays inaccessible.
g.text: Access levels are preserved: Public to Public, Protected to Protected

g.data-narration: Protected inheritance reduces visibility: both public and protected members become protected in the derived class.
g.text: Outside world cannot access any inherited members

g.data-narration: Private inheritance makes all members private in the derived class. The base class becomes an implementation detail.
This table shows exactly how each base class member type is transformed under different inheritance types.
The using declaration allows changing access levels of inherited members in derived classes.
g.text: Use using to restore or change access levels!

g.data-narration: Constructors run from base to derived, destructors run in reverse order. This ensures proper initialization and cleanup.
In multiple inheritance, each base class can have different access specifiers. The derived class inherits from multiple bases.
Lets summarize: Private never accessible in derived, Protected accessible only in derived, Public accessible everywhere.
For exams: Remember the access table, understand private is never accessible, and practice identifying access levels in code.
g.text: 1. Memorize the access control table for all inheritance types
2. Private members are NEVER accessible in derived classes
3. Protected equals Private plus Derived access
5. Constructor order: Base to Derived, Destructor: Derived to Base
